coredata.dc:title: Synergistic effect of oxygen and nitrogen functionalities for graphene-based quantum dots used in photocatalytic H2 production from water decomposition
coredata.prism:copyright: Copyright © 2015 Elsevier Ltd. All rights reserved.
coredata.dc:creator: Yeh, Te-Fu
Chen, Shean-Jen
Teng, Hsisheng
coredata.dc:description: Surface intact nitrogen-doped graphene oxide quantum dots (iNGO-QDs), which are based on the abundant elements C, H, O, and N, exhibit outstanding photocatalytic performance in H2 production through water decomposition. In this study, we synthesize the iNGO-QDs by thermally treating graphene oxide sheets in NH3 with subsequent ultrasonic exfoliation. The iNGO-QDs exhibit sizes of 2 to 6nm and consist of approximately 3 graphitic layers. Nitrogen doping in QD synthesis repairs the vacancy-type defects of GO and introduces n-type conductivity to compensate for the unbalanced charges on p-type GO, thereby suppressing leaks of photogenerated charges. Coexistence of p- and n-domains in the iNGO-QDs may form photochemical diodes to facilitate charge separation. Under visible light illumination, the iNGO-QDs steadily catalyze the production of H2 from an aqueous solution containing triethanolamine. When deposited with Pt as the cocatalyst, the iNGO-QDs exhibit high activity in H2 generation at an apparent quantum yield of 12.8% under monochromatic light (420nm) irradiation. The high activity of the iNGO-QDs can be attributed to the synergistic effect of the oxygen and nitrogen functionalities in facilitating charge separation and transfer.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Water-splitting
Graphene oxide
Graphene quantum dot
Hydrogen production
Photocatalysis
Photosynthesis

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Chemical and physical structures of the QDs
Optical and electronic properties of the QDs
Photocatalytic performance of QDs in the production of H2 through decomposition of water

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: Ministry of Science and Technology, Taiwan
xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: Ministry of Science and Technology, Taiwan

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This research was supported by the Ministry of Science and Technology, Taiwan ( 101-2221-E-006-243-MY3 , 101-2221-E-006-225-MY3 , 103-3113-E-006–009 , and 102-3113-E-006-002 ), and by the Ministry of Education, Taiwan , the Aim for the Top University Project to the National Cheng Kung University.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: XPS and Raman spectra of the QD photocatalysts. (a) Full-range XPS spectrum of iNGO-QDs, (b) C 1s XPS spectrum of iNGO-QDs, (c) N 1s XPS spectrum of iNGO-QDs and (d) Raman spectrum of iGO-QDs and iNGO-QDs.

Optical properties of iNGO-QDs. (a) Optical absorption spectrum of iNGO-QDs dispersed in water. The inset shows the photograph of the iNGO-QD solution, which is red-brown in color. (b) Plots of (αE)2 and (αE)1/2 against photon energy (E) for the iNGO-QD solution, where α is the absorbance.

Variation of capacitance (C) with the applied potential in 2M H2SO4 presented in the Mott–Schottky relationship for the iNGO-QD electrode. The capacitance was determined by electrochemical impedance spectroscopy. The symbols E Fn and E Fp represent Fermi levels of the p- and n-type conductivity domains. The inset shows an equivalent circuit comprising the space-charge capacitors of the p- and n-domains connected through ohmic contact. C p and C n are the space-charge capacitance values of individual p- and n-domains, respectively.

Schematic energy level diagrams of iNGO-QDs exhibiting p- and n-type conductivities in comparison with the potentials for water reduction and oxidation. The arrows depict the vectorial transition of the photogenerated electrons involved in the production of hydrogen.

The current−potential curve for the iNGO-QD film immersed in 2M H2SO4 solution under chopped illumination using simulated AM1.5 solar light of 100mWcm−2. The potential scan was performed at a linear rate of 5mVs−1. The inset shows the focused view of positive potential regime. The photocurrent changed from n-type anodic current to p-type cathodic current at ca. 0.05V (vs. Ag/AgCl) along the cathodic scan from 0.5 to −0.3V (vs. Ag/AgCl).
Time courses of the production of H2 using 0.9g of iNGO-QDs and iGO-QDs suspended in 200mL of 10vol% TEOA aqueous solution under visible light irradiation (420nm<λ<800nm) at 39mWcm−2.
EPR spectra of the iNGO-QDs and iGO-QDs obtained in the dark and under mercury-lamp irradiation. The solid lines represent the iNGO-QD signals and the dashed lines represent the iGO-QD signals.

Time course involved in the production of H2 from the 10vol% TEOA aqueous solution containing the 3wt% Pt-deposited iNGO-QDs over a period of 6 days, with several interventions to perform system evacuation. The system was irradiated by visible light (420nm<λ<800nm) at 39mWcm−2.
Approaches for manipulating the electronic structure of graphene: size modulation and chemical modification.
The NH3 treatment along with heating repaired the vacancy-type defects in iGO-QDs through substitutional nitrogen doping, forming the quaternary nitrogen atoms in the iNGO-QDs.

(O 1s)/(C 1s) and (N 1s)/(C 1s) atomic ratios determined from the full-range XPS spectrum (Figure 1a), carbon bonding composition determined from the C 1s XPS (Figure 1b), and nitrogen bonding composition determined from the N 1s XPS (Figure 1c) for iNGO-QDs.
Apparent quantum yields (AQYs) of previously reported photocatalysts in H2 production from aqueous solutions containing sacrificial alcohols and triethanolamine under visible light irradiation.

head.ce:title: Synergistic effect of oxygen and nitrogen functionalities for graphene-based quantum dots used in photocatalytic H2 production from water decomposition

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemical Engineering and Research Center for Energy Technology and Strategy, National Cheng Kung University, Tainan 70101, Taiwan

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Chemical Engineering and Research Center for Energy Technology and Strategy, National Cheng Kung University

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Center for Micro/Nano Science and Technology, National Cheng Kung University, Tainan 70101, Taiwan

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Center for Micro/Nano Science and Technology, National Cheng Kung University

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Engineering Science, National Cheng Kung University, Tainan 70101, Taiwan

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Engineering Science, National Cheng Kung University

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author at: Department of Chemical Engineering and Research Center for Energy Technology and Strategy, National Cheng Kung University, Tainan 70101, Taiwan. Fax: +886 6 2344496.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Surface intact nitrogen-doped graphene oxide quantum dots (iNGO-QDs), which are based on the abundant elements C, H, O, and N, exhibit outstanding photocatalytic performance in H2 production through water decomposition. In this study, we synthesize the iNGO-QDs by thermally treating graphene oxide sheets in NH3 with subsequent ultrasonic exfoliation. The iNGO-QDs exhibit sizes of 2 to 6nm and consist of approximately 3 graphitic layers. Nitrogen doping in QD synthesis repairs the vacancy-type defects of GO and introduces n-type conductivity to compensate for the unbalanced charges on p-type GO, thereby suppressing leaks of photogenerated charges. Coexistence of p- and n-domains in the iNGO-QDs may form photochemical diodes to facilitate charge separation. Under visible light illumination, the iNGO-QDs steadily catalyze the production of H2 from an aqueous solution containing triethanolamine. When deposited with Pt as the cocatalyst, the iNGO-QDs exhibit high activity in H2 generation at an apparent quantum yield of 12.8% under monochromatic light (420nm) irradiation. The high activity of the iNGO-QDs can be attributed to the synergistic effect of the oxygen and nitrogen functionalities in facilitating charge separation and transfer.

Nitrogen-doped graphene oxide quantum dots, which are based on the abundant elements C, H, O, and N, exhibit a single-crystalline structure consisting of one to three graphene sheets. The quantum dots, when deposited with Pt as cocatalyst for photocatalytic water splitting, steadily catalyze H2 production from an aqueous solution containing triethanolamine at a high quantum yield under visible light illumination.

ce:section.ce:para: The use of solar light to catalyze the cleavage of water into hydrogen for use as fuel is a sustainable and environment-friendly approach to overcome issues related to increasing energy consumption [1–6]. Ideally, a photocatalyst should enable light absorption across a wide band of the solar spectrum and should have adequate conduction band minimum (CBM) and valence band maximum (VBM) levels as well as a high degree of stability in order to sustain water redox reactions [5]. A variety of organic as well as inorganic semiconductors have been used as photocatalysts [7–10]. Organic semiconductors enable facile engineering of the electronic structure by varying the constituents of the conjugated systems [11–13]; however, they are susceptible to photocorrosion.

Carbon nitride [14] and graphene oxide (GO) [15–17] have a crystal structure similar to that of inorganic materials but exhibit electronic properties similar to those of organic conjugated systems. As a result, they have emerged as an alternative to conventional photocatalysts. These materials remain stable under irradiation by light and are easily modifiable through chemical doping to vary their electronic properties. In particular, GO sheets have a unique 2D structure that allows extensive modification through substitutional and/or surface-transfer doping treatments [7,18]. This study succeeded in synthesizing stable GO photocatalysts capable of splitting water to produce H2 without the need for cocatalysts [15,16,19,20].

In the synthesis of GO photocatalysts, the tunable electronic properties of graphene are particularly important. These adjustments can be made via size modulation and chemical modification (see Scheme 1) [21–23]. The bonding π (VBM) and antibonding π (i.e., π⁎; CBM) orbitals of graphene degenerate in the Brillouin zone corners, which renders graphene a zero band-gap material [24,25]. Novoselov et al. were the first to form graphene into QDs using electron-beam lithography, which resulted in materials with a finite band gap and demonstrated the quantum confinement effect in graphene with lateral dimensions of less than 100nm [21]. Scheme 1 reveals that band-gap creation can also be achieved through chemical modification, resulting in the formation of sp3 carbon orbitals that disrupt the sp2 conjugated network of graphene. For example, the CO covalent bonds in GO break the extended sp2 conjugated network and confine the π-electrons within the isolated sp2 domains, resulting in a finite band gap [26,27]. This kind of chemical functionalization (also known as surface transfer doping) withdraws or donates electrons to create p- or n-type conductivity, respectively, on the functionalized graphene [23]. In addition to surface transfer doping, the substitutional doping of heteroatoms into the graphene lattice opens the band gap by destroying the symmetry of the two carbons in the graphene unit cell [28]. The valence number of the doped heteroatoms (relative to that of carbon) determines the type of conductivity in the doped graphene [23,29,30].

In graphene sp2 domains with conjugated π orbitals, photogenerated molecular excitons (known as Frenkel excitons) have large Coulombic binding energy (due to a small material dielectric constant), making them prone to recombination [31]. The probability of charge separation governs the performance of photocatalysis systems using π-conjugation in the light-absorbing media. Analogous to biological photosynthesis [32], forming a photochemical diode, which comprises p- and n-type semiconductors connected through ohmic contact, is generally adopted to dissociate Frenkel-type excitons [33]. The 2D feature of graphene, which is directly susceptible to chemical modification, provides a platform for the creation of electronic domains and junctions to induce vectorial displacement and the subsequent separation of opposite charges in Frenkel-type excitons. Furthermore, the substitutional doping of heteroatoms in the honeycomb lattice of graphene can be used to repair vacancy defects and compensate for unbalanced charges caused by surface transfer doping [34].

In previous studies, we demonstrated the ability of GO and nitrogen-doped GO to catalyze water-splitting under illumination, even in the absence of cocatalysts [15,16,19,20]. The surface defects created during GO synthesis may serve as charge-trapping states, which facilitate water redox reactions [35]. However, surface defects also provide a center for charge recombination, which reduces the quantum efficiency of photocatalytic water-splitting [20]. In the present study, we prepared surface-intact nitrogen-doped GO quantum dots (iNGO-QDs) using a mild oxidation process to minimize the density of surface defects on the QDs. Lacking the surface state required to facilitate charge injection into the solution phase, the iNGO-QDs must undergo surface treatment with a cocatalyst to achieve photocatalytic water splitting. The iNGO-QD photocatalyst in this study features a synergistic combination of oxygen and nitrogen functionalities capable of catalyzing the generation of H2 from water with an apparent quantum yield of 12.8% under monochromatic irradiation at λ=420nm.

GO was prepared using a natural graphite powder (Bay carbon, SP-1, USA) according to a modified version of Hummers׳ method [36]. Graphite powder (5g) and NaNO3 (2.5g; Merck, Germany) were introduced to a solution of concentrated H2SO4 (18M, 115mL; Wako, Japan) in an ice bath. KMnO4 (15g; J.T. Baker, USA) was gradually added with stirring with the temperature of the mixture held below 20°C. The mixture was then stirred at 35°C for 4h to allow oxidation. Deionized water (230mL) was then slowly added to the mixture and stirred at 98°C for 15min. The mixture was further diluted to 700mL and stirred for 30min. The reaction was concluded with the addition of H2O2 (12mL, 35wt%; Shimakyu, Japan) while being stirred at room temperature. Multiple washings were conducted using deionized water (3×500mL). The precipitate of the final slurry was dried at 40°C for 24h to obtain the GO specimens.

Nitrogen-doped graphene (NG) was synthesized by treating the as-prepared GO in a flow of NH3 gas at 500°C for 3h. The iNGO-QDs were obtained from NG using a mild oxidation process, in which NG (0.1g) was oxidized in concentrated HNO3 (30mL) with sonication at room temperature for 12h before being heated to 140°C in an oven with evacuation to remove the residual HNO3 (boiling point of 83°C). The product was dispersed in deionized water and centrifuged to form an iNGO-QD suspension red-brown in color. A dried powder of iNGO-QD could be obtained by freeze-drying. In addition to iNGO-QDs, we also synthesized nitrogen-free iGO-QDs in the same manner, except that the NH3 treatment was replaced by Ar treatment.

X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS; Kratos AXIS Ultra DLD, UK) with Al Kα radiation was used to quantitatively analyze the chemical composition of iNGO-QDs. The C 1s and N 1s spectra were decomposed into several peaks that were fitted using a Gaussian function. Raman spectra were recorded using a DXR Raman microscope (Thermo Fisher Scientific Inc., USA) at room temperature under a laser with an excitation wavelength of 532nm. High-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HRTEM; Jeol 2100F, Japan) was used to explore the microstructure of iNGO-QDs. The samples were deposited on a carbon substrate for measurements. We also analyzed the topography of iNGO-QDs using atomic force microscopy (AFM; Nanoscope IIIa, Digital Instrument, USA) conducted in tapping mode. The samples used in AFM measurements were deposited on mica substrates. The optical absorption spectrum of an iNGO-QDs/water suspension was obtained by placing the solution in a 1-cm quartz cuvette for analysis using a Hitachi U-4100 (Japan) spectrophotometer. The chemical composition of iNGO-QDs was analyzed using an elemental analyzer (Heraeus Elementar vario EL III & CHN-OS-Rapid, Germany). Electron paramagnetic resonance (EPR) spectra were recorded on a Bruker model E-580 spectrometer (Germany) with a 400W mercury-lamp (Oriel Instrument, USA) as the light source.

QD electrodes for electrochemical analysis were prepared by drop-casting QD/water suspensions onto fluorine-doped SnO2 (FTO) conducting glass substrates (TEC 8, Hartford Glass Co., USA). The QD electrodes were subjected to electrochemical analysis in 2M H2SO4 solution with a Pt foil counter and an Ag/AgCl reference. The type of conductivity in the QD electrodes was determined using impedance spectroscopy (Zahner IM6e, Germany) in conjunction with Thales software. Measurements involved the application of a sinusoidal potential perturbation of small amplitude (10mV), superimposed on a fixed DC potential varying within a window from −0.8 to 0.8V (vs. Ag/AgCl). In the same electrochemical system, linear potential scanning (5mVs−1) was conducted to determine the CBM and VBM of the iNGO-QD specimen. Photocurrent−potential characteristics were recorded under illumination with a solar simulator (Newport SP91160A, USA) at 100mWcm−2 (AM 1.5G) using an electrochemical analyzer (CH Instrument 614B, USA).

Photocatalytic reactions were conducted at approximately 25°C in a gas-enclosed side irradiation system. The QD catalysts were suspended in a 10vol% triethanolamine (TEOA) aqueous solution in a Pyrex vessel with side irradiation from a 300W Xenon lamp (Oriel Instruments, model 66901, USA). The wavelength of the incident light was limited to 420−800nm using a UV-cutoff filter (Asahi Speatra, XUL0422, USA) and an IR-cutoff filter (Asahi Spectra, XIS0810, USA). For quantum yield measurements, we used a 420-nm band-pass filter (Newport, 20BPF10-420, USA) to obtain monochromatic irradiation. The intensity of the light irradiated on the reacting system was obtained using a photodetector (Oriel Instrument, model 71964, USA). The amount of H2 produced was determined using gas chromatography (Hewlett-Packard 7890, USA; molecular sieve 5A column, thermal conductivity detector, argon carrier gas).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Chemical and physical structures of the QDs
ce:section.ce:para: Figure 1 presents the XPS spectra of the iNGO-QDs. The full-range iNGO-QD spectrum (Figure 1a) presents the binding energy peaks of C 1s at 286eV, N 1s at 400eV, and O 1s at 532eV. Quantitative analysis of the XPS spectrum revealed that the atomic ratios of (O 1s)/(C 1s) and (N 1s)/(C 1s) were 55% and 6.3%, respectively ( Table 1). Figure 1b presents the focused C 1s spectrum ranging from 280 to 292eV. This spectrum consists of peaks at 284.6, 285.8, 286.5, 288.1, and 289.0, attributed to the CC, CN, CO, CO, and OCO groups, respectively. The high contribution from the CO carbonyl group reflects the fact that the QDs were formed by oxidizing graphitic domains to create aligned epoxy groups, which subsequently converted to carbonyl groups with a rupture of the underlying CC bonds [37]. The N 1s spectrum, ranging from 392 to 408eV (Figure 1c), comprises peaks corresponding to pyridine-like (398.4eV), pyrrolic-like (399.1eV), and quaternary (400.7eV) nitrogen atoms [20,38]. Table 1 illustrates that most of the nitrogen atoms replaced graphitic carbon atoms to form pyridine-like and quaternary nitrogen functionalities and only one-third of the nitrogen atoms were located in pyrrolic-like pentagons. Substitutive nitrogen doping in the aromatic graphene lattice may have repaired the vacancy defects present in the graphene oxide sheets [34].

Raman spectroscopy revealed how nitrogen doping affected the defects in the iNGO-QDs. Figure 1d presents a comparison of the Raman spectra of the iNGO-QD and iGO-QD powders. The iGO-QDs were synthesized in the same manner as the iNGO-QDs, but with no nitrogen doping. The spectra of the iNGO-QDs and iGO-QDs exhibited similar intensity ratios in the D and G bands (approximately 2.2), indicating that the synthesis processes produced similar amounts of sp3-type defects in these two types of QDs. The iNGO-QDs exhibited suppression in the D׳ band (1657cm−1), which was activated by vacancy-type defects [34,39]. This indicates that the NH3 treatment repaired the vacancy-type defects through substitutional nitrogen doping (see Scheme 2), thereby removing the deformation of the carbon-bond in the QD graphitic domains [34]. Substitutional nitrogen doping may have created donor levels in the QDs due to the fact that nitrogen atoms have a larger valence number than do carbon atoms (Scheme 1). The Raman spectra of both QD samples (Figure 1d) present the appearance of the 2D band (2728cm−1), indicating that the QDs comprise orderly stacked sheets of graphene with fewer than three layers.

Based on the XPS, Raman, and TEM analyses, Figure S1 of the Supporting information presents the conceptual schematic of the iNGO-QDs, in which the main body comprises orderly stacked graphene sheets of 1−3 layers. The nitrogen atoms embedded in the graphene sheets were introduced by the NH3 treatment, whereas the oxygen functionalities decorating the graphitic basal plane or periphery were introduced in the oxidative rupture for iNGO-QD production. These chemical dopings may create n- or p-type domains in the QDs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Optical and electronic properties of the QDs
ce:section.ce:para: Figure 3a presents the optical absorption spectrum of the aqueous solution containing iNGO-QDs, which exhibited absorption onset at approximately 600nm. The iNGO-QDs appeared red-brown in color in the solution (see the inset of Figure 3a). Plotting the square and square root of the absorption energy (αE, where α is the absorbance and E the photon energy) against E enabled the determination of direct and indirect band-gap energies, respectively (Figure 3b). We obtained a gap energy of 2.6eV for direct transition and 2.2eV for indirect transition for the iNGO-QDs through the application of linear extrapolation. The gap nature of graphene changes from direct to indirect gap with an increase in the oxidation level [40]. There was uncertainty about which transition mechanism existed in the system. Indirect gap transitions are likely to occur in iNGO-QDs due to their high O/C ratio (Table 1).

We determined the CBM level of the iNGO-QDs by applying cathodic potential scanning to an iNGO-QD film soaked in a 2M H2SO4 aqueous solution. An abrupt cathodic current was detected when the potential was applied above the CBM to form an inversion layer (Figure S2 Supporting information). The iNGO-QDs exhibited a CBM level of −0.8V (vs. Ag/AgCl), which was more negative than that used to generate H2 through water reduction (−0.2V vs. Ag/AgCl). iNGO-QDs are a suitable medium for catalyzing the generation of H2 through the reduction of water. We estimated that the VBM level of the iNGO-QDs is 1.4V (vs. Ag/AgCl) by using a band gap of 2.2eV, as determined by the calculation of indirect transition. This VBM level is somewhat insufficient for water oxidation (1.0V vs. Ag/AgCl) due to a small overpotential.

We used electrochemical impedance spectroscopy in conjunction with the Mott–Schottky equation (see Supporting information) to identify the conductivity type of the iNGO-QDs [41]. Figure 4 presents the capacitance values of the space charge region obtained at various applied potentials. According to the Mott−Schottky equation (a linear relationship of 1/C 2 versus applied potentials), two linear regions can be observed. The straight line with a positive slope indicates n-type conductivity, while the negative slope in the linear region demonstrates p-type conductivity. The Fermi levels were determined from the Mott−Schottky plot with the intercepts of the extrapolated straight lines on the abscissa, which are −0.7 and 0.3V (vs. Ag/AgCl) for the n- and p-type conductivities, respectively. By summarizing the data from the optical and electrochemical measurements, we were able to construct an energy level diagram for the iNGO-QDs, as shown in Figure 5. This figure also depicts the potentials for the evolution of H2 and O2 through water decomposition. Without nitrogen doping, the iGO-QD film exhibited only p-type conductivity (see Figure S3), which was due to the presence of oxygen functionalities. The coexistence of p- and n-type conductivities among the iNGO-QDs can be attributed to the surface transfer doping of oxygen functionalities resulting in the formation of p-domains and substitutional doping of nitrogen to form n-domains in single iNGO-QDs (Scheme 1). Composites consisting of individual p- and n-type semiconductors have also been shown to exhibit the p- and n-type semiconducting behaviors coexisting in the Mott−Schottky plots [42,43]. The n-domains created on the iNGO-QDs may donate electrons to compensate for the unbalanced charge in p-type GO main body, thereby suppressing leaks of photogenerated charges.

In the Mott–Schottky plot in Figure 4, the capacitance values of the space-charge regions in the n- and p-domains were equal at the crossing point of the positive and negative slopes. The crossing point (0.05V vs. Ag/AgCl) represents the potential for the changeover of the dominant conductivity-type of the iNGO-QDs. Figure 4 indicates that the apparent conductivity type of the iNGO-QDs is dominated by the domains exhibiting small capacitance (i.e., large reciprocal capacitance values). This dependence of conductivity-type on capacitance led to the conclusion that the p- and n-domains in the iNGO-QDs are connected in series (see the inset of Figure 4), resulting in the development of apparent capacitance (C) for the iNGO-QDs according to 1/C=1/C p+1/C n, where C p and C n are the capacitance values of individual p- and n-domains, respectively. Figure 6 presents the current−potential curve for the iNGO-QD film under chopped illumination using simulated AM1.5 solar light. A linear potential scan from 0.5 to −0.3V (vs. Ag/AgCl) was performed at a linear rate of 5mVs−1. The iNGO-QD film exhibited a cathodic photocurrent response at potentials more negative than 0.05V (vs. Ag/AgCl), indicative of the p-type conductivity of the iNGO-QD film. At potentials more positive than 0.05V (vs. Ag/AgCl), the current−potential curve (see the focused view in the inset of Figure 6) exhibits an anodic photocurrent response, which was contributed by the n-type conductivity in the iNGO-QD film. P-type photocurrent was more prominent than n-type, which reflects the dominant p-type characteristics of the iNGO-QDs. The coincidence of conductivity-type changeover potentials determined by the Mott−Schottky and photocurrent measurements provides evidence that the nitrogen doping in the synthesis of iNGO-QDs introduced n-type conductivity to the p-type GO lattice.

This study further varied the nitrogen content of the QDs to conclusively determine the existence of the “ambipolar” transport characteristics stemming from the oxygen functional groups and nitrogen doping. We adjusted the NH3 flow rate in the synthesis of QDs and produced specimens having XPS (N 1s)/(C 1s) ratios of 5.3% and 8.0% (designated as iNGO5-QDs and iNGO8-QDs, respectively). The iNGO-QDs have a ratio (6.3%) in between those of the iNGO5-QDs and iNGO8-QDs. Figure S4 (Supporting information) presents the Mott–Schottky plots for the iNGO5-QDs and iNGO8-QDs, also depicting the ambipolar transport characteristics of these QDs. The potentials for conductivity-type changeover were 0.15 and −0.05V (vs. Ag/AgCl) for the iNGO5-QDs and iNGO8-QDs, respectively, encompassing the value for the iNGO-QDs (0.05V vs. Ag/AgCl). The changeover potential shifted negatively as the degree of nitrogen doping in the QDs increased. This trend confirms that the nitrogen doping introduces n-type conductivity to intrinsically p-type GO and produces the ambipolar transport characteristics for the iNGO-QDs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Photocatalytic performance of QDs in the production of H2 through decomposition of water
ce:section.ce:para: This study used a gas-enclosed system with external visible light irradiation (420nm<λ<800nm) to analyze the photocatalytic performance of the iNGO-QDs and iGO-QDs with regard to the generation of H2 through the decomposition of water. Figure 7 illustrates that both the iNGO-QDs and iGO-QDs steadily catalyzed the production of H2 from an aqueous solution containing 10vol% TEOA as the sacrificial electron donor. The activity of the iNGO-QDs was four times higher than that of the iGO-QDs. The elimination of defects through nitrogen doping may have caused the high activity of the iNGO-QDs. In addition, the doping of nitrogen resulted in the embedding of n-type domains in the intrinsically p-type GO framework, thereby suppressing charge leaks.

The local doping of graphene with heteroatoms [44] or light-induced carriers (electrons or holes) transferred from semiconductors to graphene [45] have been reported to induce p−n junctions in a single-crystalline graphene sheet. With respect to the iNGO-QDs, the equivalent circuit presented in the inset of Figure 4 indicates that the coexistence of p- and n-type conductivities resulted in the formation of p−n type photochemical diodes, in which the p- and n-domains are connected through ohmic junctions. It is probable that the sp2 clusters in the GO framework served as ohmic junctions. In this diode configuration, photo-generated majority carriers of domains with distinct conductivity recombine at the p−n interface, thereby making minority carriers available for chemical reaction at the solid–solution interface [20,32]. The energy diagram in Figure 5 depicts the vectorial transition of the photogenerated electrons involved in the production of hydrogen, which can account for the high activity of the iNGO-QDs relative to the iGO-QDs. Figure S5 illustrates the strong photoluminescence of the iNGO-QDs under visible light excitation, providing further evidence of the diode characteristics of the iNGO-QDs.

Figure 8 presents the EPR spectra of the iNGO-QDs and iGO-QDs. In the dark, both QDs exhibited Lorentzian lines centered at a g value of 2.0032, which signaled the presence of unpaired electrons on the π-conjugated aromatic rings [46]. Under mercury-lamp irradiation, the iNGO-QDs exhibited a larger enhancement in the EPR signal than did the iGO-QDs, demonstrating the effectiveness of the iNGO-QDs in the formation of separated charges under illumination. This result reveals that the iNGO-QDs, which consisted of isotype diode heterostructures, are capable of facilitating charge separation, and therefore functioning as stable metal-free photocatalysts for the light-driven production of H2. Nevertheless, with such a diode configuration for charge separation (Figure 5), the injection of photogenerated electrons from the iNGO-QDs into the solution may present a bottleneck governing the evolution of H2.

The in situ deposition of Pt as a cocatalyst (or an electron sink) substantially increased the activity of the iNGO-QDs and iGO-QDs in the photocatalytic H2 evolution. The iNGO-QDs were much more active than were the iGO-QDs after Pt deposition, further confirming the synergistic effect of oxygen and nitrogen functionalities in the iNGO-QDs. The deposition of 3wt% Pt proved optimal in improving the activity of the iNGO-QDs (Figure S6). Figure 9 presents the time course involved in the production of H2 from the TEOA solution containing the 3wt% Pt-deposited iNGO-QDs over a period of 6 days, with several interventions to perform system evacuation. H2 steadily evolved in the system with little decay in activity. The total amount of H2 produced after 6 days of irradiation was ca. 2.2mmol, which far exceeded the amount of loaded Pt nanoparticles (ca. 0.14mmol). The high turnover (t), defined as t=(molar quantity of evolved H2×2)/(molar quantity of used Pt), demonstrates the stable photocatalytic reaction associated with H2 evolution, rather than photocorrosion of the QD surface.

Figure S7 (Supporting information) presents the time course of the production of H2 using the 3wt% Pt-deposited iNGO-QDs under monochromatic irradiation at 420nm. The intensity of the 420-nm monochromatic irradiation was 0.487mWcm−2 and the irradiated area was 12.56cm2. The apparent quantum yield (AQY) in the production of H2 at 420nm was calculated using the following equation:
AQY =
number
of
evolved
H
2
molecules × 2
number
of
incident
photons
× 100 % .

The incident photon flux was calculated to be 76.4μmolh−1. The rate of H2 production was 4.9μmolh−1, which corresponds to an AQY of 12.8%. This quantum yield is superior to that of carbon nitrides [47]. as well as most metal-containing photocatalysts suspended in aqueous solutions containing sacrificial alcohols or TEOA ( Table 2) [47–55]. These results demonstrate the promising role of graphene-based QDs as media for solar fuel production, with the tuning of oxygen and nitrogen functionalities to enhance light absorption and charge separation.

We also tested the robustness of the 3wt% Pt-deposited iNGO-QDs under severe light irradiation using a mercury lamp. The production of H2 remained stable throughout the 48-h illumination period (Figure S8). The number of hydrogen atoms in the evolved H2 was 3.2 times the number of carbon atoms in the iNGO-QDs. The atomic ratio of H/C in the iNGO-QDs was approximately 0.2/1, confirming that the evolved H2 was derived from the photocatalytic splitting of water. The stable trend in the production of H2 confirmed the robustness of the iNGO-QDs in photocatalytic water splitting.

This paper presents a facile method for the preparation of graphene-based photocatalysts for the production of H2 through water decomposition. The synthesized iNGO-QDs, when deposited with Pt as the cocatalyst, catalyzed the production of H2 from a TEOA aqueous solution, presenting a quantum yield of 12.8% under monochromatic light (420nm) irradiation. The activity of the iNGO-QDs proved superior to that of previously reported photocatalysts comprising C, H, O, and N, and was among the most effective of all photocatalysts, including metal-containing materials. The high activity of the iNGO-QDs can be ascribed to the synergistic effects of the oxygen and nitrogen functionalities, which include the creation of band gap to facilitate light absorption, the repair of vacancy defects resulting in the suppression of charge leaks, and the construction of photochemical diodes to facilitate vectorial electron transport. We believe that this facile approach to synthesizing graphene-based QDs offers a new avenue for the construction of other heteroatom-doped GO-QDs with suitable light-absorbing spectra for photocatalysis. The great diversity of chemical compositions and electronic structure created through heteroatom doping could advance the application of graphitic materials in the field of solar fuel generation.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This research was supported by the Ministry of Science and Technology, Taiwan (101-2221-E-006-243-MY3, 101-2221-E-006-225-MY3, 103-3113-E-006–009, and 102-3113-E-006-002), and by the Ministry of Education, Taiwan, the Aim for the Top University Project to the National Cheng Kung University.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found in the online version at doi:10.1016/j.nanoen.2015.01.021.

ce:biography.ce:simple-para: Te-Fu Yeh received his B.S. and M.S. degrees in Chemical Engineering from National Cheng Kung University, Taiwan, in 2009 and 2011, respectively. He is currently on a doctorate course at National Cheng Kung University in the Department of Chemical Engineering under the supervision of Professor Hsisheng Teng. His research interests focus on the synthesis and design of nano-structured graphene and graphene-oxide materials for application in photocatalysis, photoluminescence, photovoltaics, and phototherapy of cancers.

Shean-Jen Chen received his B.S. degree from National Taiwan University in 1987 and his M.S. degree in Mechanical Engineering from Columbia University in 1991. In December 1996, he was awarded his Ph.D. degree for research in adaptive noise cancellation and image restoration at the University of California, Los Angeles (UCLA). He is currently a Distinguished Professor at the Engineering Science Department of National Cheng Kung University of Taiwan and is actively engaged in researching Advanced Nonlinear Optical Microscopy and Three-dimensional Photolithography.

Hsisheng Teng received his B.S. degree in Chemical Engineering from National Cheng Kung University, Taiwan, in 1984 and his Ph.D degree in Engineering from Brown University, USA, in 1992. Dr. Teng joined the Department of Chemical Engineering of National Cheng Kung University in 1997 as an Associate Professor. He is currently a University Chair Professor of Chemical Engineering at National Cheng Kung University. His research interests include energy conversion and storage, with particular focus on photovoltaic solar cell, photocatalytic water splitting, supercapacitor, lithium ion battery, and application of graphene-based materials.
